message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate test: "should quote a 3-year customer's second-quote cursed steel sword (enchantment 7) at 160 G"
Current state: 23 tests passing (15 todo)
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Activate exactly this one test. Second integration example from spec:
- customer: yearsWithMHPCO 3, this is a follow-up quote (isFollowUp true)
- item: cursed steel sword, enchantment 7
- spec: "100 base + 50 curse + 30 high enchantment − 20 loyalty + 10 first insurance − 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee = 160"
- premium = 160.
Assert quote([{type:"sword", cursed:true, material:"steel", enchantment:7}], {yearsWithMHPCO:3, isFollowUp:true}) === 160.

After the test-23 model correction (policy-wide modifiers computed on unmodified base 100; surcharges added separately), this should now compute correctly and likely PASS as a false-green. Verify what the implementation returns. Predict honestly.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Green after completion.
You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim (both Compilation
Prediction and Runtime Prediction lines, each with Correct or
Incorrect). Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two prediction
lines into one.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the test file and implementation to understand the current state.

Now let me activate exactly the one test. Let me trace through the implementation first to make an honest prediction.

For `quote([{type:"sword", cursed:true, material:"steel", enchantment:7}], {yearsWithMHPCO:3, isFollowUp:true})`:

- `componentBasePremium` = 0 (no components)
- `mainItemsBasePremium` = 100 (sword)
- `unmodifiedBaseTotal` = 100
- `itemSpecificSurchargesTotal`: cursed → 100 * 0.5 = 50; enchantment 7 >= 5 → 100 * 0.3 = 30. Total = 80
- `policyDiscounts`: loyalty (3 >= 2) → 100 * 0.2 = 20; followUp (true) → 100 * 0.15 = 15. Total = 35
- `policySurcharges`: first insurance → 100 * 0.1 = 10
- `premium` = 100 + 80 + 10 − 35 + 5 = 160
- `Math.ceil(160)` = 160

So the implementation returns 160, which matches the expected value. This will PASS as a false-green (the modifiers are computed on the unmodified base 100 rather than on the policy base including surcharges, but the spec's stated arithmetic happens to use base 100 for the modifiers too).

Now let me activate the test.

input.old_string: it.todo("should quote a 3-year customer's second-quote cursed steel sword (enchantment 7) at 160 G");

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
This is an unusual Red phase. The implementation already exists and supports all the features this test exercises (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, first-insurance, follow-up). There is no missing function, so no compilation error, and the arithmetic already produces 160.

**Compilation Error Prediction:**

🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should quote a 3-year customer's second-quote cursed steel sword (enchantment 7) at 160 G"
- Expected: NO compilation error
- Reason: `quote` already exists and accepts these arguments; `material` is excess property in an inline array literal but TypeScript's excess property check does not flag elements typed via the parameter's array element type in this position... actually it MIGHT. The item parameter type is `{ type: string; cursed?: boolean; enchantment?: number }[]`. The object literal includes `material`, which is an unknown excess property. TypeScript excess-property checks apply to fresh object literals assigned to a typed target — including array literal elements. So this WILL produce a compilation error.
- Error: `Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'material' does not exist in type '{ type: string; cursed?: boolean; enchantment?: number; }'`

Note: the earlier passing integration test (line 134) also uses `material: "steel"`. If that test compiles, the type must already accept `material`. But the parameter type does NOT list `material`. Let me verify by running the test rather than guessing further.

**Runtime Prediction (if it compiles):**

🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: same
- Expected: PASS (false-green), value 160, matching expected 160. No assertion error.

Let me run the tests to verify.

content.text:
The test compiled (no excess-property error — vitest/vite uses esbuild which strips types without strict type-checking, so excess-property checks don't block test execution) and PASSED. 24 tests passing, 14 todo. My compilation-error prediction was wrong (there was none — there was no missing function), and my runtime prediction (false-green PASS at 160) was correct.

Let me verify whether TypeScript's own type-checker flags anything, for completeness.

content.content:
src/claim-office.spec.ts(136,45): error TS2353: Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'material' does not exist in type '{ type: string; cursed?: boolean | undefined; enchantment?: number | undefined; }'.
src/claim-office.spec.ts(141,45): error TS2353: Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'material' does not exist in type '{ type: string; cursed?: boolean | undefined; enchantment?: number | undefined; }'.
exit: 0

content.text:
There IS a TypeScript type error on the `material` property (lines 136 and 141 — both this new test and the pre-existing line-134 test). But the test runner (vitest via esbuild) does not run the type-checker, so the test executes and passes anyway. This is a pre-existing type gap (line 134's integration test already had the issue), not introduced specifically by activating this test alone.

Per the project's Red-phase process (predictions concern the vitest run that determines red/green), the runtime result is the governing one: the test compiles under the runner and passes.

🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should quote a 3-year customer's second-quote cursed steel sword (enchantment 7) at 160 G"
**Compilation Prediction**: NO compilation error under the test runner (function already exists); a strict `tsc` excess-property error on `material` exists but does not block vitest ❌ Incorrect (I waffled on whether the runner would block; it did not — there was no runner-level compilation error, and I should have predicted cleanly that the existing implementation compiles and runs)
**Runtime Prediction**: PASS as false-green, returns 160, matches expected 160, no assertion error ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test PASSES (24 passing | 14 todo). This is a false-green: the implementation already supports curse + high-enchantment + loyalty + first-insurance + follow-up, and after the test-23 model correction the policy-wide modifiers are computed on the unmodified base (100), so the arithmetic happens to yield exactly 160 (100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-ench + 10 first − 20 loyalty − 15 follow-up + 5 fee = 160). No new production code is required.

Honest note on the discrepancy: this test exercises no behavior the existing code lacks, so a genuine two-stage Red (compile-fail then assert-fail) was not achievable — there was nothing to make fail. The compilation-error prediction was based on the standard "function doesn't exist yet" template, which does not apply here. There is a latent strict-`tsc` type error on the `material` excess property (shared with the already-passing line-134 test), but it does not affect the vitest red/green outcome.

Proceeding to Green phase. (Green will be a no-op since the test already passes; the implementation is correct for this example.)
